reasoning: C and D both nail the core structural move: they separate the goal (civic value) from the proxy metric (foot traffic), name the question-substitution trap explicitly, and insist success be measured as value-delivered rather than footfall. C edges ahead with the sharpest calibration (explicitly held 'priors held loosely', a black-hat day-shelter risk it designs around rather than wishes away, and a warning that early enthusiasm can flatter direction 4) plus the strongest single synthesized bet (combine verification desk + library-of-things, both reusing paid-for assets). D matches C's framing rigor (Meadows leverage levels, two-stocks distinction, premortem on all four reverting to 'another reason to come read') but is marginally less concrete on test mechanics. B is strong and well-reasoned via the 'monopoly' lens and gives a clear cheapest-first verdict, but accepts the foot-traffic frame more than C/D and surfaces fewer counter-traps. A offers solid, defensible directions and the clearest political-legibility argument, but it asserts causes without checking them, gives thinner uncertainty handling, and never interrogates the foot-traffic metric itself.
